---
title: "Exploring China's Global and Regional Health and Educational Data"
author: "Presented by: Group 05"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    width: 1400
    height: 900
    css: styles.css
title-slide-attributes: 
  data-background-image: 23833.jpg
editor: visual
execute:
  echo: false
---
## Data Licence Information

### Life Expectancy
Human Mortality Database (2025); UN, World Population Prospects (2024) – processed by Our World in Data

### Fertility Rate
UN, World Population Prospects (2024) – processed by Our World in Data

### Air Pollution
World Health Organization - Global Health Observatory (2025) – processed by Our World in Data

### Water Contamination
WHO/UNICEF Joint Monitoring Programme for Water Supply, Sanitation and Hygiene (2025) – Learn more about this data CC BY

### Doctor to Patient Ratio
Global Health Workforce Statistics, WHO, via World Bank (2026) – processed by Our World in Data

WHO Mental Health Atlas 2020 via UNICEF (2023) – with major processing by Our World in Data

### Mental Health
World Health Organization (2024) – with major processing by Our World in Data

World Health Organization (2024)

### Literacy
UNESCO Institute for Statistics (2025) – with minor processing by Our World in Data

### Gender Equality in Education
UNDP, Human Development Report (2025) – with minor processing by Our World in Data

UNDP, Human Development Report (2025) – with minor processing by Our World in Data

### Post-Secondary Education

```{r}
#Importing physicians csv file
doctors_per_1000 <- read.csv("physicians-per-1000-people.csv")

#Cleaning column names with janitor()
doctors_per_1000 <- (doctors_per_1000)

#Changing name of final column
names(doctors_per_1000)[4] <- "physicians"

```

```{r}
#Filtering out the rows of countries we don't want to use. They're not "Giants" economically
table(doctors_per_1000$Entity)

target_ents<- c("United States", "China", "Japan", "India", "United Kingdom")

doctors_per_1000 <- doctors_per_1000 |> dplyr::filter(Entity %in% target_ents)

doctors_per_1000 <- doctors_per_1000 |> dplyr::mutate(Entity = forcats::fct_reorder(Entity, physicians))

range(doctors_per_1000$Year, na.rm = TRUE)
sum(is.na(doctors_per_1000$Year))

doctors_per_1000 <- doctors_per_1000 |> 
  dplyr::mutate(Year = as.numeric(Year)) |> 
  dplyr::filter(Year > 2010)
#Left with all of the "Giant" Countries with data 
```

```{r}
#Calculating percentage difference between China 2010 and China 2020

china_2011 <- doctors_per_1000 |>
  dplyr::filter(Entity == "China", Year == 2011) |>
  dplyr::pull(physicians)

china_2020 <- doctors_per_1000 |>
  dplyr::filter(Entity == "China", Year == 2020) |>
  dplyr::pull(physicians)

((china_2020 - china_2011) / china_2011) * 100
```

## Where Does China sit amoung the worlds largest economies in terms of healthcare workforce density?

```{r}
#Visualising this data in a bar chart
ggplot2::ggplot(doctors_per_1000, ggplot2::aes(x = factor(Year), y = physicians, fill = Entity)) + ggplot2::geom_col(position = "dodge") + ggplot2::labs(x = "Year", y = "Physicians", title = " Number of Physicians in Economically Giant Countries Over the Years", fill = "Country", caption = "Data Source: Global Health Workforce Statistics, WHO, via World Bank (2026) – processed by Our World in Data") + ggplot2::theme(axis.text.x = ggplot2::element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1)) + ggplot2::scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Spectral")
```

This Chart reveals that China's Grown in physician density between 2011 and 2020 was remarkable. However, compared to the other "economic giants" there is a significant difference in amounts. Starting the decade with the lowest recorded density amoug the giants, China consistently expanded its medical workforce, almost doubling its figures by 2020 (60.6% increase).

Despite this, China still lags behind the United States and the United Kingdom, who sustain higher physician-to-population ratios. While China has overtaken India and (almost) Japan, it's "giant" economic status is not yet matched by the per-capita healthcare resources seen in Western peers, suggesting a continuing need for investment in the medical workforce.

## How does China compare with selected countries in post-secondary educational attainment?

```{r echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)

edu <- read.csv(
  "share-of-the-population-with-a-completed-post-secondary-education.csv",
  check.names = FALSE
)

compare_data <- edu %>%
  filter(
    Entity %in% c("China", "Canada", "Saudi Arabia", "United Kingdom"),
    Year >= 2000,
    Year <= 2020
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Entity = factor(Entity, levels = c("China", "Saudi Arabia", "United Kingdom", "Canada"))
  )

ggplot(compare_data, aes(x = Year, y = Value, color = Entity)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.5, alpha = 0.95) +
  geom_point(size = 1) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2000, 2020, by = 5)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  labs(
    title = "Population aged 25+ with completed post-secondary education ",
    subtitle = "(2000–2020)",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Share of population",
    color = "Country"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 18) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16, hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12, hjust = 0.5, color = "grey30"),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 15),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 12, color = "grey20"),
    legend.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 12),
    legend.position = "right",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_line(linewidth = 0.4, color = "grey85")
  )
```


It is evident that China maintained a high literacy rate throughout this time period, performing at a level comparable to the strongest countries in the dataset. Unlike indicators such as post-secondary education, literacy shows much less separation between China and the comparison countries.

This suggests that China has been highly effective in delivering standard education to its population. Overall, the chart highlights literacy as a relative strength for China, demonstrating that foundational educational access is far more developed in this nation.

## How has China’s scientific and technical journal article output changed compared with selected major economies from 2000 to 2022?

```{r echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)

articles <- read.csv(
  "scientific-and-technical-journal-articles.csv",
  check.names = FALSE
)

names(articles)[4] <- "journal_articles"

compare_data <- articles %>%
  filter(
    Entity %in% c("China", "United States", "Germany", "India", "Saudi Arabia"),
    Year >= 2000,
    Year <= 2022
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Entity = factor(Entity, levels = c("China", "United States", "Germany", "India", "Saudi Arabia"))
  )

ggplot(compare_data, aes(x = Year, y = journal_articles, color = Entity)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 1.8) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2000, 2022, by = 5)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_comma()) +
  labs(
    title = "China compared with selected major economies",
    subtitle = "(2000–2022)",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Number of journal articles",
    color = "Country",
    caption = "Data Source: World Bank / Our World in Data"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16, hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5, color = "grey30"),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 9, color = "grey20"),
    legend.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 9),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 8),
    legend.position = "right",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_line(linewidth = 0.3, color = "grey85")
  )
```


China experienced the fastest growth in scientific and technical journal article output over the period shown. While the United States started as the leading publicist, China expanded rapidly and overtook it in the later years. Despite increasing their output, Germany and India only showed moderate development, and Saudi Arabia remained at a lower level throughout. Overall, the chart shows that China has become a leading global producer of scientific research.
